metadata.title: New York Review of Books homepages in 2022

nybooks-2022.text:
J.W. McCormack						
						Kafka’s Inkblots
						What can be read in the trove of sketches and doodles saved by Franz Kafka’s literary executor?

						    July 19, 2022

Sarah Blackwood						
						Spiritual Exercises
						In her most recent graphic novel, Alison Bechdel explores her lifelong obsession with fitness.

						    July 21, 2022 issue

FT					
					Apropos of Nothing
					Nonbeing belongs to that category of concepts that seem self-evident and self-explanatory, but have been objects of deepest perplexity for philosophers.

					    July 21, 2022 issue

Robert Kuttner						
						Free Markets, Besieged Citizens
						Why did Democratic presidents embrace an economic credo that annihilated their own public philosophy and its appeal to the electorate?

						    July 21, 2022 issue

Verlyn Klinkenborg						
						The Forest’s-Eye View
						Two new books investigate the ways in which deforestation affects climate change, and climate change affects forests.

						    July 21, 2022 issue

Aaron Matz                                        
                                        Flaubert’s Planet
                                        Do novelists, and their readers, bear some responsibility for the climate crisis?

                                            July 21, 2022 issue

Deborah Eisenberg                                        
                                        Condemned to Life
                                        The austerely drawn world of Jacqueline Harpman’s novel I Who Have Never Known Men provides a richly allusive consideration of human life.

                                            July 21, 2022 issue

Rosa Lyster                                        
                                        Whose Homeland?
                                        Damon Galgut’s novel The Promise confronts South Africa’s most significant political issue: the land and whom it belongs to.

                                            July 21, 2022 issue

Verlyn Klinkenborg                                        
                                        The Forest’s-Eye View
                                        Two new books investigate the ways in which deforestation affects climate change, and climate change affects forests.

                                            July 21, 2022 issue

Ursula Lindsey                                        
                                        A Painful In-Betweenness
                                        Syrian novelists attempt to balance the obligation to not let the world forget what happened in their country with their doubts that telling stories of the war will make a difference to its victims.

                                            July 21, 2022 issue

Namwali Serpell                                            
                                            ‘She’s Capital!’
                                            The figure of the Whore is everywhere in art, her beauty to be gazed upon and admired. With the Internet, has she finally found her own artistic terrain?

                                                July 21, 2022 issue

Daniel Immerwahr                                            
                                            Wielding Wheat
                                            A new history makes a case for the world-ordering power of wheat.

                                                July 21, 2022 issue

Miranda Seymour                                            
                                            ‘Turbulence and Misery’
                                            Anguish, provoked by her acute sense of physical and social inadequacy, was essential for Jean Stafford’s literary talent to flourish.

                                                July 21, 2022 issue

Helon Habila                                            
                                            Crude Reality
                                            Imbolo Mbue’s How Beautiful We Were confronts the often ignored messiness and violence of oil extraction.

                                                July 21, 2022 issue

“The hunting of dangerous beasts, lions above all, served the Assyrian kings, and later those of Persia, as a display of power and dominion; large game parks were established in capital cities to allow them to demonstrate their prowess.”
                        
                        — James Romm: Kings of the Universe

Christine Henneberg            
            Aspirations
            As an abortion provider, what I give my patients is not just a procedure but the space to make their own decisions about their bodies.

                May 5, 2022

Robert L. Tsai and Mary Ziegler            
            The New Abortion Vigilantism
            The abortion bounty law is novel and thus far unique to Texas, but similar civil enforcement regimes have a much darker history.

                September 23, 2021

Jill Filipovic, an interview with Rebecca Gomperts            
            Choice by Mail
            With Roe v. Wade under threat, abortion rights in the US are facing their greatest challenge yet. Enter the Dutch doctor who has opened a new front with safe medication on demand.

                January 11, 2022

Sue Halpern            
            How Republicans Became Anti-Choice
            These days, the litmus test for Republican politicians and judges is opposition to abortion. This was not always the case.

                November 8, 2018 issue

Lucy Ives            
            A Line that Wants to Speak
            The Beat artist Rick Barton, a fixture of the San Francisco café scene, filled notebooks with expressive line drawings that are only now getting their due.

                July 12, 2022

Ishmael Reed            
            The Buffalo I Knew
            The city is at a crossroads. Which path will it take?

                July 9, 2022

Tim Parks            
            A Text Adrift
            How does the death of the author change the task of the translator?

                July 7, 2022
Gary Saul Morson: Dostoevsky and His Demons

Shane McCrae                            
                            The White People in My Blood
                        
                            July 21, 2022 issue

Ben Lerner                            
                            Meridian Response
                        
                            July 21, 2022 issue

Geoff McFetridge, interviewed by Leanne Shapton            
            The Edge of Legibility
            “Design and commercial art can be like assembling a sculpture out of found objects; building something unusual out of the familiar.”

                July 16, 2022

Rumaan Alam, interviewed by Daniel Drake            
            Reading in the Bath
            “I read to be lifted out of my own life, which I thought boring.”

                July 9, 2022

Laura Kolbe, interviewed by Lauren Kane            
            One Mind Humming
            “In my practice I seek to have it both ways: to respect patients and my own mind enough to believe that we can meet in the open field of conversation with our mutual intuitions, experiences, and bodies of knowledge.”

                July 2, 2022

Ishmael Reed                                    
                                    The Buffalo I Knew
                                        July 9, 2022

David Cole                                    
                                    The Supreme Court’s Unprecedented Turn
                                        July 8, 2022

Gordon F. Sander                                    
                                    Memory Wars in Latvia
                                        July 21, 2022 issue

                                    Condemned to Life
                                        July 21, 2022 issue

                                    The Master of Blame
